Improving How You Ask Questions in Communities
This is a follow-up to "Master the Art of Asking — Stop Asking Roastable Questions". This one's focused on asking questions in communities, since that's where most asking happens.
Before reading this, I'd want you to read the previous post first: "
Let's Talk About Questions That Really Deserve a Roasting
It already covers 80%+ of the core points:
How to reply after getting an answer
OK, assuming you've read it. Let's get into the main topic.
Mind the character of the venue
What do I mean by "character of the venue"? We're in a community-explosion era. Discord, LINE communities, Facebook groups — these chat apps let you get answers fast.
But why mind the venue's character?

Because every community has its own best way to be asked questions. LINE communities, for instance, don't have threading like Slack or Discord. So your question gets buried by other chat traffic super easily, or interrupted.
Big LINE communities easily have hundreds or thousands of people chatting. Drop a question in and it scrolls off — because you're not the only one talking.
So before asking, check whether the venue can "stay focused" on a discussion.
If it can't, I'd recommend collecting your question fully before sending. Otherwise if you message it line by line, you might get:

Person A: Hi everyone, I have some questions
Person B: I think Kenting is super fun!
Person C: Yeah super fun, also lots of pretty girls!
Person D: And lots of sports cars there!
...dozens of messages later
Good Samaritan: (replying to A) What's your question?
Person A: My code's spacing isn't showing up
Person D: The parking spacing near the B&B isn't too tight either
Person A: Hi everyone, I have some questions
Person B: I think Kenting is super fun!
Person C: Yeah super fun, also lots of pretty girls!
Person D: And lots of sports cars there!
Good Samaritan: (replying to A) What's your question?
Person A: My code's spacing isn't showing up
Person D: The parking spacing near the B&B isn't too tight either
See? The venue can't "stay focused" on a single topic — your question gets interrupted by other messages.
Better: send your question all at once:

Person A: Hi everyone, I have some questions about why my code's spacing isn't showing up
Person B: I think Kenting is super fun!
Person C: Yeah super fun, also lots of pretty girls!
Person D: And lots of sports cars there!
...dozens of messages later
Good Samaritan: (replying to A) The issue is your display setting is wrong.
Person D: The parking spacing near the B&B isn't too tight either
Person A: Hi everyone, I have some questions about why my code's spacing isn't showing up
Good Samaritan: (replying to A) The issue is your display setting is wrong.
Sending it all in one go gives way more focused discussion than line-by-line messages. So before asking, check whether the venue can "stay focused" — and if not, send your question in one message.
A lot of times we share the code we think is buggy, but if you can, use online services to reproduce it.

The main reason: your dev environment might differ from the helper's, and during the helping process, results might not reproduce, so they can't give a clear answer. Then they have to spend extra effort understanding your environment.
Also, when I'm helping, I want to be able to run the code directly, not spend time on environment troubleshooting. I might just skip your question.
So to boost reply rate, learn an online service like Codepen or CodeSandbox. If you don't know how, no worries — I have tutorials:
Get Started with CodePen in 10 Minutes (with Chinese subtitles)
Get Started with CodeSandbox in 10 Minutes (with Chinese subtitles)
Online services significantly improve your reply rate.
I don't want to download your code and find the dev environment differs from mine, then bounce back and forth on env questions. The back-and-forth alone is enough for me to skip.
This is serious. As I said in "
Helpers are not your personal tutors.

So respect whoever's willing to help. People are passing through and pointing you in a direction out of goodwill. That doesn't mean "they" should become your personal tutor and hand over private contact info. Unless they
share their contact info, don't DM them or ask for their contact.
So when asking in a community, avoid:
Can I DM you later? I'll upload the source code soon and ask you.
Could you share your contact info? I'll come back to you after some changes.
Do this, and you might not see that person answering you next time.
"Unless the helper proactively shares their contact info, don't ask for it."
"Unless the helper proactively says you can DM them, never say you're going to DM them."
This is part of asking respectfully. A lot of community participants are anonymous — some genuinely don't want to share personal contact info, or be disturbed.
After getting an answer, don't delete
If the venue can "stay focused" on discussion, please don't delete your question after getting an answer.
The reason: "Your question is probably a problem someone else will hit too."

If you delete after getting an answer, the next person who hits the same issue will ask it again. That problem becomes a "monthly thread" — keeps being asked, keeps being answered, keeps being deleted. That helps no one and frustrates helpers.

Also, if you keep asking + getting answers + deleting threads, eventually nobody will help you, because your question doesn't stay around for reference. You may worry your question is dumb, but that "dumb" question is exactly what helps other beginners. So if possible...
the thread and find their own answer.

In a community, every person is a potential teacher. Don't ignore someone because their explanation didn't click for you, or keep @-tagging the one person whose explanations clicked. That's disrespectful of their time.
If you do this, you'll find that person leaves all your messages on read.
Because the implicit message is: "Is there nobody else in this community who can answer? Does your project being buggy somehow obligate me to answer?"
So don't designate a specific person. You posted in the community — why are you also designating who must reply? If that's what you want, just ask your coworker or manager directly.
Calling asking a skill or art is accurate, because:
"A good question gets solved fast. A bad one sinks like a stone."
So mastering how to ask matters a lot — otherwise we should all just open a "Psychic Academy".
